metadata.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

one_liner.via: PagedAttention algorithm and paged KV cache memory management
one_liner.because: it eliminates memory fragmentation through block-level management and enables memory sharing across decoding algorithms like parallel sampling and beam search

claim_tree.statement: Existing systems struggle to serve LLMs at high throughput because the key-value cache memory for each request is huge and grows and shrinks dynamically.

children.statement: The authors propose PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by the classical virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems.

claim.statement: The authors propose PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by the classical virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory efficiency enables higher throughput
universal_facets.analogy_source: virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems

algorithm_facets.g2: Attention computation with virtual memory paging techniques

children.statement: The improvement is more pronounced with longer sequences.
The authors build vLLM, an LLM serving system that achieves near-zero waste in KV cache memory and flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests.

claim.statement: The authors build vLLM, an LLM serving system that achieves near-zero waste in KV cache memory and flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Reduces KV cache memory waste to increase throughput without increasing latency.
universal_facets.analogy_source: virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems

systems_facets.s1_resource: KV cache memory
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV cache -> Physical memory pages via paging
systems_facets.s5_policy: UNSPECIFIED
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: vLLM improves the throughput of popular LLMs by 2-4 × with the same level of latency compared to the state-of-the-art systems, such as FasterTransformer and Orca.
PagedAttention divides the request's KV cache into blocks, each containing a fixed number of tokens, which are not necessarily stored in contiguous space.

evidence.artifact_id: Fig. 5: Figure 5. Illustration of the PagedAttention algorithm,

claim.statement: PagedAttention divides the request's KV cache into blocks, each containing a fixed number of tokens, which are not necessarily stored in contiguous space.

systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: KV cache blocks containing fixed number of tokens
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Request KV cache blocks mapped to non-contiguous physical memory via paging
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocating blocks on demand and preemptive request scheduling
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPUs with sufficient memory for KV cache

children.statement: vLLM uses block-level memory management and preemptive request scheduling, and supports models exceeding the memory capacity of a single GPU.

claim.statement: vLLM uses block-level memory management and preemptive request scheduling, and supports models exceeding the memory capacity of a single GPU.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory fragmentation reduction vs. throughput improvement
universal_facets.analogy_source: operating system virtual memory with paging

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory, specifically KV cache memory for LLM serving.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Request KV cache -> Non-contiguous memory blocks via PagedAttention paging.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Preemptive request scheduling.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU hardware accelerators for LLM inference serving.

children.statement: vLLM improves throughput without affecting model accuracy, with improvements more pronounced for larger models and more complex decoding algorithms.

evidence.artifact_id: Fig. 17: Figure 17. Performance on chatbot workload.
Fig. 17: Fig. 17 shows that vLLM can sustain 2 × higher request

children.statement: vLLM can sustain 2 × higher request rate compared to baselines.
vLLM can sustain 2 × higher request
On the ShareGPT dataset, vLLM sustains 1.7x–2.7x higher request rates compared to Orca (Oracle) and 2.7x–8x compared to Orca (Max), while maintaining similar latencies.
Compared to FasterTransformer, vLLM can sustain up to 22x higher request rates.
vLLM sustains 2 × higher request rates compared to three Orca baselines on the ShareGPT chatbot workload using OPT-13B.
vLLM adopts a centralized scheduler to coordinate the execution of distributed GPU workers.

claim.statement: vLLM adopts a centralized scheduler to coordinate the execution of distributed GPU workers.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: UNSPECIFIED

claim.statement: The KV cache manager manages the KV cache in a paged fashion, enabled by PagedAttention.

children.statement: The KV cache manager manages the physical KV cache memory on the GPU workers through the instructions sent by the centralized scheduler.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: vLLM organizes the KV cache as fixed-size KV blocks, analogous to virtual memory pages, allowing contiguous logical blocks to map to non-contiguous physical memory.

claim.statement: vLLM organizes the KV cache as fixed-size KV blocks, analogous to virtual memory pages, allowing contiguous logical blocks to map to non-contiguous physical memory.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: dynamic allocation vs reserved memory

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU DRAM memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV block maps to physical KV block via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation without pre-reserving all positions in advance.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with DRAM memory for caching.

children.statement: A block engine allocates contiguous chunks of GPU DRAM and divides them into physical KV blocks, which can also be swapped to CPU RAM.

claim.statement: A block engine allocates contiguous chunks of GPU DRAM and divides them into physical KV blocks, which can also be swapped to CPU RAM.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Dynamic allocation vs. pre-reserved memory waste

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU DRAM and CPU RAM for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Fixed-size physical KV blocks representing logical KV blocks.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block tables.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation without pre-reserving all positions in advance.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU DRAM and CPU RAM support for swapping.

children.statement: The KV block manager maintains block tables that record the mapping between logical and physical KV blocks and the number of filled positions for each request.

claim.statement: The KV block manager maintains block tables that record the mapping between logical and physical KV blocks and the number of filled positions for each request.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Dynamic allocation eliminates memory waste vs pre-reserved contiguous memory

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU DRAM memory used for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block tables.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation without reserving all positions in advance.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with contiguous GPU DRAM memory.

children.statement: Separating logical and physical KV blocks allows vLLM to dynamically grow KV cache memory without reserving it for all positions in advance, eliminating most memory waste.
vLLM reserves only the necessary KV blocks to accommodate the KV cache generated during prompt computation, rather than reserving memory for the maximum possible generated sequence length initially.

claim.statement: vLLM reserves only the necessary KV blocks to accommodate the KV cache generated during prompt computation, rather than reserving memory for the maximum possible generated sequence length initially.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Hardware utilization vs memory fragmentation

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate physical blocks dynamically based on generated KV cache size.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory space required for block engine allocation.

children.statement: The ShareGPT dataset has 8.4 × longer input prompts and 5.8 × longer outputs on average than the Alpaca dataset, with higher variance.
For OPT-13B on the ShareGPT dataset, vLLM processes 2.2x more requests at the same time than Orca (Oracle) and 4.3x more requests than Orca (Max).
vLLM maps logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks and updates the block table's #filled record during decoding.

claim.statement: vLLM maps logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks and updates the block table's #filled record during decoding.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: memory fragmentation vs hardware utilization

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate new physical block when logical block is full.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with physical GPU memory.

children.statement: vLLM concatenates all input tokens of the current iteration (prompt phase requests and latest tokens for generation phase requests) as one sequence and feeds it into the LLM during batching.

claim.statement: vLLM concatenates all input tokens of the current iteration (prompt phase requests and latest tokens for generation phase requests) as one sequence and feeds it into the LLM during batching.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: reserving memory for max sequence length
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Block size vs memory fragmentation

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Fill left to right, allocate new physical block when all previous blocks are full.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory supporting PagedAttention kernel access.

children.statement: Storing multiple tokens within a KV block (block size > 1) enables the PagedAttention kernel to process the KV cache across more positions in parallel, increasing hardware utilization and reducing latency.
vLLM limits memory waste for a request within one block by filling blocks from left to right and allocating new physical blocks only when previous blocks are full.

claim.statement: vLLM limits memory waste for a request within one block by filling blocks from left to right and allocating new physical blocks only when previous blocks are full.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: memory fragmentation vs utilization

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory reserved for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Logical KV blocks and physical KV blocks.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Fill blocks left to right, allocate new physical block only when full.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory with PagedAttention kernel support.

children.statement: vLLM maps the logical blocks of multiple sequences to different physical blocks within the space reserved by the block engine, allowing non-contiguous physical memory usage.

claim.statement: vLLM maps the logical blocks of multiple sequences to different physical blocks within the space reserved by the block engine, allowing non-contiguous physical memory usage.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Hardware utilization versus memory fragmentation

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory reserved for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate new physical block when logical block is full, filling left to right.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory with block engine support.

children.statement: vLLM implements a copy-on-write mechanism at the block granularity for physical blocks modified by multiple sequences in parallel sampling.
We show 6.1% - 9.8% memory saving on parallel sampling and 37.6% - 55.2% on beam search on the Alpaca dataset.
In the same experiments with the ShareGPT dataset, we saw 16.2% - 30.5% memory saving on parallel sampling and 44.3% - 66.3% on beam search.
vLLM introduces a reference count for each physical block to manage sharing across multiple logical blocks in parallel sampling.

claim.statement: vLLM introduces a reference count for each physical block to manage sharing across multiple logical blocks in parallel sampling.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory sharing reduces usage but requires copy-on-write for writes

systems_facets.s1_resource: Memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical blocks mapped to physical blocks via reference count.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Reference count tracking and copy-on-write on modification.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: vLLM manages KV blocks for beam search candidates by sharing physical blocks among candidates and reducing memory copy overhead.

claim.statement: vLLM manages KV blocks for beam search candidates by sharing physical blocks among candidates and reducing memory copy overhead.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory sharing efficiency vs write isolation overhead

systems_facets.s1_resource: KV cache memory managed via physical and logical blocks.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Physical KV block and logical KV block.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV block maps to physical KV block via PagedAttention.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Reference count tracking and copy-on-write mechanism for block modification.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: Previous LLM serving systems require frequent memory copies of the KV cache across beam candidates, resulting in significant overhead.
children.rejected_what: Frequent memory copies of the KV cache across beam candidates in previous systems
children.rejected_why: High overhead compared to vLLM's physical block sharing

children.statement: The improvement of vLLM over Orca (Oracle) on OPT-13B and the Alpaca dataset goes from 1.3 × in basic sampling to 2.3 × in beam search with a width of 6.
In beam search, vLLM applies the copy-on-write mechanism only when newly generated tokens fall within an old shared block.

claim.statement: In beam search, vLLM applies the copy-on-write mechanism only when newly generated tokens fall within an old shared block.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory sharing reduces copies; CoW handles writes.

systems_facets.s1_resource: Memory space for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV block maps to Physical KV block.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Copy-on-write applied when new tokens fall within old shared block.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: vLLM reserves physical blocks for predefined shared prefixes and maps user logical blocks to these cached physical blocks with the last block marked copy-on-write.
vLLM achieves 1.67 × higher throughput than Orca (Oracle) when the one-shot prefix is shared.
vLLM achieves 3.58 × higher throughput than Orca (Oracle) when 5 examples are shared.
vLLM uses a common mapping layer that translates logical blocks to physical blocks to facilitate simultaneous processing of requests with different decoding preferences.
Existing systems cannot efficiently process requests with different decoding preferences simultaneously.
children.rejected_what: Efficient simultaneous processing of requests with different decoding preferences in existing systems
children.rejected_why: Existing systems cannot efficiently do this

children.statement: vLLM adopts the first-come-first-serve (FCFS) scheduling policy for all requests to ensure fairness and prevent starvation.

claim.statement: vLLM adopts the first-come-first-serve (FCFS) scheduling policy for all requests to ensure fairness and prevent starvation.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory bandwidth versus computation power for eviction decisions

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks for KV cache and CPU memory for swapped blocks.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: GPU physical blocks, sequences, and sequence groups for scheduling.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence KV cache maps to GPU blocks or CPU memory via swapping.
systems_facets.s5_policy: First-come-first-serve scheduling and all-or-nothing eviction policy.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory and CPU RAM with bandwidth between them.

children.statement: vLLM preempts the latest requests first and serves the earliest arrived requests first.

claim.statement: vLLM preempts the latest requests first and serves the earliest arrived requests first.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: CPU RAM bandwidth vs GPU computation power

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory blocks for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Sequence or sequence group of KV cache blocks.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence blocks mapped to GPU or CPU memory via swapping or recomputation.
systems_facets.s5_policy: FCFS scheduling with latest-first preemption and all-or-nothing eviction.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU memory with bandwidth for swapping.

children.statement: vLLM implements an all-or-nothing eviction policy for sequences because all blocks of a sequence are accessed together.

claim.statement: vLLM implements an all-or-nothing eviction policy for sequences because all blocks of a sequence are accessed together.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: CPU swap bandwidth vs GPU computation power

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory blocks for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: KV cache blocks within a sequence.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence blocks mapped to GPU or CPU memory blocks via swapping.
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction policy for sequences.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU memory with sufficient bandwidth.

children.statement: Sequences within one request (e.g., beam candidates) are gang-scheduled as a sequence group and preempted or rescheduled together.

claim.statement: Sequences within one request (e.g., beam candidates) are gang-scheduled as a sequence group and preempted or rescheduled together.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: standard eviction policies and classic virtual memory techniques
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory bandwidth vs. computation power for recovery

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks for storing KV cache.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Sequence group containing multiple sequences within a request.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequences mapped to GPU blocks via gang-scheduling.
systems_facets.s5_policy: First-come-first-serve for requests, all-or-nothing for sequences.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory available for KV cache with CPU swap.

children.statement: vLLM uses swapping to recover evicted blocks by copying them to CPU memory, managed by a CPU block allocator.

claim.statement: vLLM uses swapping to recover evicted blocks by copying them to CPU memory, managed by a CPU block allocator.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: CPU-GPU bandwidth vs computation power for latency

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks and CPU memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: KV cache blocks and sequences within sequence groups.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical blocks mapped to CPU memory via swapping mechanism.
systems_facets.s5_policy: FCFS scheduling and all-or-nothing eviction policy for sequences.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU RAM with bandwidth between them.

children.statement: Swapping incurs excessive overhead with small block sizes due to numerous small data transfers between CPU and GPU limiting PCIe bandwidth, while recomputation is more efficient.
Swapping is more efficient when the block size is large.
For medium block sizes from 16 to 64, the two methods exhibit comparable end-to-end performance.
vLLM uses recomputation to recover evicted blocks by regenerating KV cache, which can be lower latency than swapping.

claim.statement: vLLM uses recomputation to recover evicted blocks by regenerating KV cache, which can be lower latency than swapping.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory bandwidth between CPU RAM and GPU memory versus GPU computation power.

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks and CPU memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: GPU physical blocks and sequences within sequence groups.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical sequences map to GPU blocks or CPU memory via swapping or recomputation.
systems_facets.s5_policy: FCFS scheduling and all-or-nothing eviction policy for sequence groups.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory and CPU RAM with bandwidth between them.

children.statement: Recomputation overhead remains constant across different block sizes.
Recomputation overhead is never higher than 20% of swapping's latency.
The number of blocks swapped to CPU RAM never exceeds the number of total physical blocks in GPU RAM.

claim.statement: The number of blocks swapped to CPU RAM never exceeds the number of total physical blocks in GPU RAM.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory capacity vs recomputation latency

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks and CPU RAM blocks for KV cache.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Evicted KV cache blocks mapped to CPU RAM blocks via swapping.
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction policy and FCFS scheduling policy.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: CPU RAM and GPU memory with sufficient bandwidth for swapping.

children.statement: vLLM supports the widely used Megatron-LM style tensor model parallelism strategy on Transformers.
On the Alpaca dataset with OPT-175B, vLLM's advantage over Orca (Oracle) and Orca (Pow2) is less pronounced because the setup is compute-bound rather than memory-bound.
vLLM features a single KV cache manager within the centralized scheduler, which is shared by different GPU workers.

claim.statement: vLLM features a single KV cache manager within the centralized scheduler, which is shared by different GPU workers.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Centralized memory management avoids synchronization overhead during execution.

systems_facets.s5_policy: UNSPECIFIED
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: Distributed GPUs

children.statement: Different GPU workers share the mapping from logical blocks to physical blocks to execute the model with physical blocks provided by the scheduler.
In model parallel execution, each GPU worker only stores a portion of the KV cache for its corresponding attention heads.

claim.statement: In model parallel execution, each GPU worker only stores a portion of the KV cache for its corresponding attention heads.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: standard distributed execution without centralized KV cache management
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Partitioned KV cache storage vs centralized memory management

systems_facets.s1_resource: KV cache memory
systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical blocks -> physical blocks via centralized scheduler mapping
systems_facets.s5_policy: UNSPECIFIED
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: Distributed GPUs

children.statement: GPU workers synchronize intermediate results via all-reduce communication without the coordination of the scheduler during execution.
GPU workers do not synchronize on memory management; they only need to receive all the memory management information at the beginning of each decoding iteration.
vLLM is an end-to-end serving system with a FastAPI frontend and a GPU-based inference engine.

claim.statement: vLLM is an end-to-end serving system with a FastAPI frontend and a GPU-based inference engine.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: The vLLM engine is written in 8.5K lines of Python and 2K lines of C++/CUDA code.

claim.statement: The vLLM engine is written in 8.5K lines of Python and 2K lines of C++/CUDA code.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: The frontend extends the OpenAI API interface, allowing users to customize sampling parameters for each request, such as the maximum sequence length and the beam width k.

claim.statement: The frontend extends the OpenAI API interface, allowing users to customize sampling parameters for each request, such as the maximum sequence length and the beam width k.

children.statement: The authors develop a fused GPU kernel that reshapes new KV cache into a block-optimized layout and writes it to positions specified by the block table in a single kernel launch to minimize launch overheads.

The authors adapt the attention kernel to read KV cache according to the block table and perform attention operations on the fly, assigning a GPU warp to read each block for coalesced memory access and supporting variable sequence lengths within a request batch.
The authors implement a kernel that batches copy operations for discontinuous blocks into a single kernel launch to mitigate overhead from using the cudaMemcpyAsync API.

claim.statement: The authors implement a kernel that batches copy operations for discontinuous blocks into a single kernel launch to mitigate overhead from using the cudaMemcpyAsync API.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Reduces copy overhead by batching operations into single kernel launch

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory bandwidth
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Discontinuous blocks -> single kernel launch via batching copy operations
systems_facets.s5_policy: UNSPECIFIED
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with warp support

claim_tree.statement: When managed inefficiently, this memory can be significantly wasted by fragmentation and redundant duplication, limiting the batch size.
On an NVIDIA A100 40GB GPU serving a 13B-parameter LLM, approximately 65% of memory is allocated for model weights and close to 30% is used for the dynamic KV cache states.

evidence.artifact_id: Fig. 1: Figure 1. Left: Memory layout when serving an LLM with

claim_tree.statement: Profiling results show that only 20.4% - 38.2% of KV cache memory is used to store actual token states in existing systems due to internal and external fragmentation.

evidence.artifact_id: Fig. 2: Figure 2. Average percentage of memory wastes in different

claim_tree.statement: Existing systems cannot exploit memory sharing opportunities for advanced decoding algorithms like parallel sampling and beam search because the KV cache is stored in separate contiguous spaces.

evidence.artifact_id: Fig. 3: Figure 3. KV cache memory management in existing systems. Three types of memory wastes – reserved, internal fragmentation,

claim_tree.statement: General model serving systems fail to account for LLM-specific autoregressive properties and token states, resulting in missed optimization opportunities.

children.statement: Orca increases throughput via request scheduling and interleaving, whereas vLLM increases throughput via memory utilization and fragmentation reduction.
children.rejected_why: Orca achieves throughput via scheduling, while vLLM achieves it by increasing memory utilization to fit more working sets into memory.

children.statement: Prior memory optimization techniques like FlexGen, OLLA, and FlashAttention lack online serving support or fine-grained block-level management.
children.rejected_why: FlexGen does not target online serving settings; OLLA does not do fine-grained block-level management or online serving; FlashAttention applies tiling and kernel optimizations rather than block-level memory management.

claim_tree.rejected_what: General model serving systems (e.g., Clipper, TensorFlow Serving, Nexus, InferLine, Clockwork, DVABatch, REEF, Shepherd, AlpaServe)
claim_tree.rejected_why: They fail to take into account the autoregressive property and token state of LLM inference.

negative_claims.statement: Previous LLM serving systems require frequent memory copies of the KV cache across beam candidates, resulting in significant overhead.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Frequent memory copies of the KV cache across beam candidates in previous systems
negative_claims.rejected_why: High overhead compared to vLLM's physical block sharing

negative_claims.statement: Existing systems cannot efficiently process requests with different decoding preferences simultaneously.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Efficient simultaneous processing of requests with different decoding preferences in existing systems
negative_claims.rejected_why: Existing systems cannot efficiently do this
